micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Principle: Benefit Received Without Expense to the Other Party

When the Fourth Wall Is Added: The Benefit Becomes Actionable

Addressing the Counterargument: Does the Beneficiary Actually Want the Fence?
Rabbi Yosi's Teaching: Payment Required Only If the Beneficiary Builds the Fourth Wall
The Implication: Ze Neheneh Ve-Ze Lo Chaser Results in Exemption
The Rebuttal: The Case Is Actually One of Loss Due to Increased Perimeter
The Watchman Alternative: How to Quantify Benefit in Ambiguous Cases

macro_segments.title: The House and Upper Story: A Paradigm Case of Benefit Without Loss?

micro_segments.title: The Scenario: Building the Ground Floor When the Upper Story Owner Needs It
Competing Inferences: Does the Beneficiary Pay Rent Once the Ground Floor Is Completed?
The First Inference: Payment Is Required Based on Rabbi Yehuda's Statement

The Rashi Resolution: The House Is Brand New, Making Deterioration a Real Injury
The Broader Principle: Even Trivial Loss Can Trigger Full Payment Obligation

macro_segments.title: Amoraim Dispute: Does Ze Neheneh Ve-Ze Lo Chaser Obligate Payment?

micro_segments.title: Rav Ami's Position: No Obligation if There Is No Loss

Rav Pappa's Observation: Rabbi Abbahu's Position Was an Inference, Not Explicit
The Me'ilah Case as Proof: Using Hekdesh Property Involves Ze Neheneh Ve-Lo Chaser

Raba's Resolution: Hekdesh Is Treated as If Known to All, So Civil Laws Apply
Tosafot's Alternative: Me'ilah and Civil Law Operate on Different Standards

Mar Bar Rav Ashi's Observation: Evidence of Sheidim in Desolate Places
